ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat dish — grilled skewered beef with sauce; closest FoodEx2 anchor for marinated grilled beef composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for beef satay with peanut sauce; A0FKP (beef meat dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for beef satay with peanut sauce as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of beef satay consists of 4 skewers (approximately 80–100g of beef) accompanied by approximately 20–30g of peanut sauce, totalling around 120g.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Satay with Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Satay with Sauce (F06040007). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Composite Foods — Analogous Estimate Based on Peanut Sauce and Marinated Beef Composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef satay with peanut sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated based on the composite profile: grilled marinated beef (negligible GI contribution), peanut sauce (GI ~14–20 for peanuts per Sydney GI Database), palm sugar (GI ~54), and the overall carbohydrate matrix of 16.97g/100g dominated by sugars from peanut sauce. The blended GI of approximately 45 is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with mixed dishes containing legume-based sauces and moderate sugar. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (45 × 16.97 × 1.2) / 100 = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 352mg/100g combined with high sugar (15.43g/100g) from peanut sauce contributing to insulin-driven sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit beef satay to 3–4 skewers per sitting, request peanut sauce on the side and use sparingly, and avoid accompanying ketupat (compressed rice) to keep total sodium and glycaemic load within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 15.43g/100g — approaching the diabetes hard gate — derived primarily from palm sugar and sweeteners in the peanut sauce, with dietary fibre of only 2.87g/100g providing limited buffering of postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat beef satay as an occasional food, strictly limit peanut sauce to a minimal dipping amount, avoid ketupat accompaniment, and monitor postprandial glucose given the high sugar content relative to fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1g/100g) from beef and coconut milk in peanut sauce, trans fat estimated at 0.1g/100g from charcoal grilling and sauce processing, and high sugar (15.43g/100g) contributing to dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 3 skewers, use minimal peanut sauce to reduce saturated fat and sugar intake, and prefer leaner protein alternatives such as chicken satay to reduce overall atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); trans fat estimated at 0.1g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory; sugar at 15.43g/100g approaches pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute chicken or tofu satay to reduce arachidonic acid load, limit peanut sauce to reduce palm sugar and saturated fat, and request oven-grilled or pan-grilled preparation to minimise advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation from charcoal grilling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef satay with peanut sauce is a popular Malay and Singaporean hawker dish consisting of marinated beef strips threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal, served with a rich, sweet-savoury peanut dipping sauce. The beef marinade typically includes turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, coriander, cumin, shallots, garlic, and palm sugar, imparting a golden-brown colour and complex aromatic flavour. The peanut sauce (kuah kacang) is made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, palm sugar, lemongrass, galangal, dried chillies, and shrimp paste, producing a thick, slightly sweet, mildly spicy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 206 kcal, 15.75g protein, 8.35g fat, and 16.97g carbohydrate, with notably high sugar at 15.43g/100g derived predominantly from the peanut sauce. Sodium is 352mg/100g and dietary fibre is 2.87g/100g. The dish is halal and high in protein but carries a high-sugar warning due to the peanut sauce composition. Clinically, the high sugar content relative to fibre raises concern for type 2 diabetes management, while sodium and saturated fat from coconut milk are relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Beef as a red meat contributes arachidonic acid relevant to inflammatory conditions. Commonly served at hawker centres, pasar malam (night markets), and festive occasions across Singapore and Malaysia, often accompanied by ketupat (compressed rice), cucumber, and raw onion.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06040007), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. All seven reported nutrients match the input record exactly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which slightly reduces overall certainty but does not affect the high confidence in macronutrient data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for beef satay with peanut sauce is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06040007 as of 2026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry F06040007 as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on estimated contribution from charcoal grilling and peanut sauce processing; this is an estimate and not a confirmed laboratory value from the HPB source. saturated_fat_g of 2.1g/100g is estimated from the beef and coconut milk components using USDA FoodData Central reference values for comparable ingredients, as the HPB entry does not report saturated fat separately. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef satay with peanut sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 16.97 × 1.20) / 100 = 9.1 for a 120g serving. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input is incorrect for a beef dish and has been removed from dietary_flags. Halal status retained as confirmed by the Malay culinary tradition and HPB classification.
